background_cases: The explanation of the fall of a stone by citing Newton's law of gravitation and the stone's initial height above the ground, from which the time and velocity of impact can be deduced.
The explanation of the boiling of water at a certain temperature by citing the relevant gas laws and the atmospheric pressure at the location.
The explanation of the period of a pendulum by citing the formula relating period to length and gravitational acceleration.
definition: An account of a phenomenon constitutes a scientific explanation if and only if (i) it deduces the phenomenon from a general law and a statement of initial conditions, (ii) the law is a well-established empirical generalisation, and (iii) the explanation renders the phenomenon nomically expectable.
conditions: deduces the phenomenon from a general law and initial conditions
law is a well-established empirical generalisation
explanation renders the phenomenon nomically expectable
counterexample: The explanation of the extinction of the non-avian dinosaurs by the Chicxulub asteroid impact does not deduce the extinction from a general law and initial conditions. There is no law of the form 'whenever an asteroid of mass m strikes a planet of type P, the following species go extinct.' The explanation describes a singular, unrepeatable event — a particular impact at a particular time — and traces its consequences through a complex chain of environmental disruptions: debris clouds, global cooling, acid rain, collapse of photosynthesis. The first condition fails, and the phenomenon is not rendered nomically expectable in any deductive sense, yet the asteroid-impact explanation is one of the most widely accepted in modern science.
abductive_insight: The background cases were drawn from classical mechanics, where phenomena are deducible from universal laws and precisely stated initial conditions. The definition abstracted deducibility, well-established law, and nomic expectability as essential features of explanation. But the dinosaur-extinction case reveals that some of the most compelling scientific explanations are historical narratives that trace the consequences of singular events through complex causal chains, without subsuming the explanandum under any general law.
passage:
Consider three cases of scientific explanation. The fall of a stone is explained by citing Newton's gravitational law and the stone's initial height, from which its time of fall and velocity of impact can be deduced. The boiling of water is explained by citing the gas laws and the atmospheric pressure. The period of a pendulum is explained by citing the formula that relates period to length and gravitational acceleration. From these cases one might extract a definition: an account of a phenomenon constitutes a scientific explanation if and only if it deduces the phenomenon from a general law and a statement of initial conditions, the law is a well-established empirical generalisation, and the explanation renders the phenomenon nomically expectable.

But this definition, drawn from the explanatory practices of classical mechanics, proves too restrictive when applied to the historical sciences. Consider the explanation of the extinction of the non-avian dinosaurs at the close of the Cretaceous period. The now widely accepted account holds that a large asteroid struck the earth at what is now the Chicxulub crater in the Yucatan Peninsula, some sixty-six million years ago, and that the environmental consequences of this impact — vast quantities of debris ejected into the atmosphere, a prolonged period of global cooling, the collapse of photosynthesis, and the disruption of food chains — led to the extinction of the dinosaurs and many other species. This explanation is supported by an impressive body of evidence: the worldwide iridium anomaly at the Cretaceous-Palaeogene boundary, the shocked quartz, the tsunami deposits, and the crater itself. Yet it does not deduce the extinction from a general law and initial conditions. There is no law that states what happens whenever an asteroid of a given size strikes a planet; the event is singular and unrepeatable. The explanation does not render the extinction nomically expectable in any deductive sense; rather, it traces the consequences of a particular event through a complex and contingent chain of environmental disruptions. The background cases were all drawn from physics, where deduction from universal laws is the norm. The definition mistook this deductive structure for the essence of all scientific explanation, overlooking the narrative explanations of the historical sciences, in which the aim is not to subsume a phenomenon under a law but to reconstruct the particular sequence of events and processes that brought it about.

background_cases: Boyle's law, which states that at constant temperature the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume — an exceptionless regularity confirmed by innumerable experiments.
Snell's law of refraction, which states that the ratio of the sines of the angles of incidence and refraction is constant for a given pair of media — a regularity holding without known exception.
Coulomb's law, which states that the electrostatic force between two point charges is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
definition: A generalisation constitutes a law of nature if and only if (i) it expresses an exceptionless regularity, (ii) it holds across all times and places, and (iii) it supports counterfactual conditionals — that is, it tells us what would happen under conditions that do not in fact obtain.
conditions: expresses an exceptionless regularity
holds across all times and places
supports counterfactual conditionals
counterexample: Mendel's laws of inheritance — the law of segregation and the law of independent assortment — are widely recognised as laws of biology, yet they are not exceptionless. Linked genes on the same chromosome violate the law of independent assortment; genetic phenomena such as meiotic drive violate the law of segregation. These exceptions are well documented and well understood. The first condition fails, yet Mendel's laws remain fundamental to genetics and are universally called laws.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all physical laws describing exact, exceptionless regularities. The definition abstracted exceptionlessness, universality, and counterfactual support as essential features. But Mendel's laws reveal that in the biological sciences, laws may describe robust tendencies that admit of systematic exceptions — exceptions that are themselves explicable by further biological principles — without ceasing to be laws.
passage:
Consider three generalisations that are regarded as laws of nature. Boyle's law states that at constant temperature the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume — a regularity confirmed by innumerable experiments without known exception. Snell's law states that the ratio of the sines of the angles of incidence and refraction is constant for a given pair of media. Coulomb's law states that the force between two point charges varies as the product of the charges divided by the square of the distance. From these cases one might define a law of nature: a generalisation constitutes a law if and only if it expresses an exceptionless regularity, holds across all times and places, and supports counterfactual conditionals.

But this definition, drawn from the exact physical sciences, encounters difficulty when applied to the laws of biology. Consider Mendel's laws of inheritance. The law of segregation states that the two alleles for a trait separate during gamete formation, so that each gamete carries only one allele. The law of independent assortment states that alleles for different traits are distributed to gametes independently of one another. These generalisations are called laws without hesitation; they are among the most fundamental principles of genetics and they transformed the study of heredity. Yet they are not exceptionless. Genes located on the same chromosome are physically linked and do not assort independently; crossing-over and recombination produce partial exceptions, and phenomena such as meiotic drive and segregation distortion violate the law of segregation itself. These exceptions are well documented, well understood, and widespread. The first condition fails. The background cases were all drawn from physics, where laws describe exact, exceptionless regularities. The definition mistook this exactness for an essential feature of all laws, when in the biological sciences, a generalisation may be recognised as a law even though it admits of systematic exceptions — exceptions that are themselves explicable by further biological mechanisms and that do not diminish the generality or importance of the law itself.

background_cases: Newton's theory of gravitation, which unified terrestrial and celestial mechanics under a single set of mathematical principles and was developed through decades of systematic investigation.

Maxwell's electromagnetic theory, which unified electricity, magnetism, and optics in a single mathematical framework, the product of sustained theoretical work building on Faraday's experimental discoveries.
Einstein's special theory of relativity, which was developed through a systematic analysis of the inconsistencies between Newtonian mechanics and Maxwellian electrodynamics.
definition: A body of propositions constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) it is the product of a deliberate programme of theoretical unification, (ii) it has been developed incrementally through the resolution of specific problems, and (iii) it was accepted by the scientific community on the basis of decisive empirical tests.
conditions: product of deliberate programme of theoretical unification
developed incrementally through resolution of specific problems
accepted on basis of decisive empirical tests
counterexample: The theory of plate tectonics was not the product of a deliberate programme of unification. It emerged piecemeal from disparate lines of evidence — Wegener's observations of continental fit, Hess's discovery of seafloor spreading, the mapping of magnetic reversals on the ocean floor, and the seismological delineation of plate boundaries — pursued by investigators in different countries and different disciplines who were not engaged in any coordinated theoretical programme. Nor was it accepted on the basis of any single decisive test; acceptance came gradually as the cumulative weight of evidence from many independent sources became overwhelming. The first and third conditions fail, yet plate tectonics is one of the great unifying theories of modern science.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all theories developed by individual geniuses or small groups working within a coherent intellectual programme. The definition abstracted deliberate unification, incremental problem-solving, and decisive testing as essential features. But plate tectonics shows that a great unifying theory may emerge unplanned from the convergence of independent lines of inquiry pursued by researchers who did not set out to construct a unified theory, and may be accepted not on the basis of a single decisive test but through the gradual accumulation of converging evidence.

From these cases one might define a scientific theory: a body of propositions constitutes a theory if and only if it is the product of a deliberate programme of theoretical unification, it has been developed incrementally through the resolution of specific problems, and it was accepted by the scientific community on the basis of decisive empirical tests.

But this definition, drawn from the great theories of mathematical physics, proves too narrow. Consider the theory of plate tectonics. It is one of the most powerful unifying theories in the history of science, explaining the distribution of earthquakes and volcanoes, the formation of mountain ranges, the opening and closing of oceans, and the geographical distribution of fossils. Yet it was not the product of a deliberate programme of theoretical unification. It emerged from disparate lines of inquiry pursued by investigators who were largely unaware of one another's work. Hess's hypothesis of seafloor spreading, proposed in 1962, drew upon oceanographic data that Wegener had never seen. The mapping of magnetic reversals on the ocean floor, carried out by Vine and Matthews, provided evidence that neither Wegener nor Hess had anticipated. The theory crystallised not through a coordinated programme but through the unexpected convergence of independent discoveries. Nor was it accepted on the basis of a single decisive test; its acceptance was gradual, driven by the cumulative weight of evidence from geology, palaeontology, seismology, and oceanography. The background cases were theories built by deliberate programmes of unification. The definition mistook this deliberateness for an essential feature of all theories, overlooking the possibility that a great unifying theory may emerge unplanned from the convergence of independent lines of evidence.

background_cases: The classification of chemical elements by atomic number in the periodic table, where each element occupies a unique position determined by a single measurable property.
The classification of geometric figures by the number of sides — triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons — where the criterion is precise and the categories are mutually exclusive.
The classification of crystalline structures by their symmetry groups, where each crystal belongs to exactly one of the thirty-two point groups.
definition: A scientific classification is adequate if and only if (i) it is based on a single measurable property of the objects classified, (ii) it assigns each object to exactly one category, and (iii) the boundaries between categories are sharp and non-arbitrary.
conditions: based on single measurable property
assigns each object to exactly one category
boundaries between categories are sharp and non-arbitrary
counterexample: The classification of stars by spectral type — the familiar sequence O, B, A, F, G, K, M — is one of the most important classifications in astrophysics. Yet it is not based on a single measurable property; it depends upon surface temperature, luminosity, and the pattern of absorption lines in the star's spectrum, which reflect its chemical composition. Moreover, the boundaries between spectral types are not sharp: stars grade continuously from one type to the next, and the dividing lines are conventional rather than dictated by nature. The first and third conditions fail, yet the spectral classification of stars is indispensable to modern astronomy.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the periodic table, geometric figures, crystal symmetry groups — were all classifications with discrete, sharply bounded categories determined by a single criterion. The definition abstracted single-property basis, unique assignment, and sharp boundaries as essential features. But stellar classification reveals that some of the most fruitful scientific classifications deal with continuously varying phenomena, employ multiple criteria, and draw boundaries that are conventional rather than natural, without thereby being arbitrary or inadequate.

The classification of geometric figures groups them by the number of sides, with precise and mutually exclusive categories. The classification of crystalline structures assigns each crystal to one of thirty-two point groups determined by its symmetry. From these cases one might define an adequate scientific classification: it must be based on a single measurable property, it must assign each object to exactly one category, and the boundaries between categories must be sharp and non-arbitrary.

But this definition, drawn from cases in which nature provides discrete, sharply bounded categories, proves inadequate when applied to classifications of continuously varying phenomena. Consider the spectral classification of stars, one of the most important taxonomic schemes in astrophysics. Stars are classified into spectral types — the familiar sequence O, B, A, F, G, K, M — on the basis of the pattern of absorption lines in their spectra, which reflects their surface temperature and chemical composition. This classification has been indispensable to the development of modern astronomy; it organises the bewildering variety of stars into a comprehensible sequence and provides the foundation for theories of stellar structure and evolution. Yet it is not based on a single measurable property. Surface temperature is the primary criterion, but luminosity, chemical composition, and the detailed pattern of spectral lines all play a role, and these criteria do not always vary in lockstep. Moreover, the boundaries between spectral types are not sharp. Stars do not fall into discrete natural kinds; they grade continuously from one type to the next, and the dividing lines between O and B, or between G and K, are matters of convention established by the astronomical community. The first and third conditions fail. The background cases were classifications of entities that come in discrete, naturally bounded categories. The definition mistook this discreteness for a universal requirement, overlooking the classifications that impose useful conventional boundaries upon continuous natural variation.

background_cases: The reduction of geometrical optics to physical optics, in which the laws of reflection and refraction are derived from the wave theory of light in the limit of short wavelengths.
The reduction of Kepler's laws of planetary motion to Newtonian mechanics, in which Kepler's three laws are derived as consequences of Newton's law of gravitation applied to a two-body system.
The reduction of the ideal gas law to the kinetic theory of gases, in which the macroscopic relation PV = nRT is derived from the statistical behaviour of a large number of molecules.
definition: A scientific theory T1 is reducible to a theory T2 if and only if (i) the entities posited by T1 are shown to be identical with entities described by T2, (ii) the laws of T1 are derivable from the laws of T2, and (iii) T1 becomes explanatorily superfluous once the reduction is complete — that is, everything T1 explains can be explained at least as well by T2 alone.
conditions: entities of T1 identical with entities of T2
laws of T1 derivable from laws of T2
T1 becomes explanatorily superfluous after reduction
counterexample: One might suppose that psychology is in principle reducible to neuroscience. But consider the psychological concept of a phobia. A phobia is characterised by its intentional content — it is a fear of something: of spiders, of heights, of enclosed spaces. The intentional content of a phobia is not identical with any particular neural state; the same phobia may be realised by different neural configurations in different individuals, and the same neural firing pattern might, in a different context, constitute a different mental state. The laws of phobic behaviour — that exposure to the feared object produces avoidance, that phobias can be acquired through conditioning and extinguished through habituation — are not derivable from neuroscience in any straightforward sense. And psychology does not become explanatorily superfluous; the psychological level of description captures patterns and regularities that are invisible at the neural level. All three conditions fail, yet the relationship between psychology and neuroscience is one of the central cases in the philosophy of reduction.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all reductions within physics, where the higher-level entities could be straightforwardly identified with lower-level entities and the higher-level laws derived from lower-level laws. The definition abstracted identity, derivability, and superfluity as essential to reduction. But the relationship between psychology and neuroscience reveals that higher-level sciences may describe patterns that are multiply realisable — implemented by different physical substrates in different cases — so that the higher-level entities cannot be identified with particular lower-level entities, the higher-level laws cannot be derived, and the higher-level descriptions remain explanatorily indispensable.

Consider three cases of reduction. The laws of geometrical optics are derived from the wave theory of light in the short-wavelength limit. Kepler's laws of planetary motion are derived from Newton's law of gravitation. The ideal gas law is derived from the kinetic theory. From these cases one might define reduction: a theory T1 is reducible to T2 if and only if the entities of T1 are identical with entities described by T2, the laws of T1 are derivable from T2, and T1 becomes explanatorily superfluous once the reduction is complete.

But this definition, drawn from reductions within physics, encounters a fundamental difficulty when applied to the relationship between psychology and neuroscience. Consider the psychological concept of a phobia — an intense, irrational fear of a specific object or situation. A phobia is characterised by its intentional content: it is a fear of something. An arachnophobe fears spiders; a claustrophobe fears enclosed spaces. Now, one might suppose that a phobia is identical with a particular neural state. But the same phobia may be realised by different neural configurations in different individuals; the precise pattern of neural activity underlying a fear of spiders may differ from one brain to another, depending upon the person's history, the structure of her neural connections, and countless other factors. The intentional content of the phobia — its being about spiders — is not captured by any single neural description. Furthermore, the psychological laws governing phobias — that exposure to the feared stimulus produces avoidance behaviour, that phobias can be acquired through classical conditioning and extinguished through systematic desensitisation — are not derivable from the laws of neuroscience. These psychological regularities hold across individuals with very different brains, and they are stated in terms that have no direct translation into the vocabulary of neurons and synapses. And psychology does not become superfluous; the psychological level of description captures generalisations that are invisible at the neural level. The background cases were reductions within physics, where entities at one level could be cleanly identified with entities at a lower level. The definition mistook this clean identification for a universal feature of reduction, overlooking the multiple realisability that makes higher-level sciences explanatorily autonomous.

background_cases: The hypothesis that all copper conducts electricity, which would be refuted by the discovery of a single piece of copper that does not conduct.
The hypothesis that water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure, which would be refuted by a single well-controlled experiment yielding a different boiling point.
The hypothesis that the speed of light in a vacuum is constant, which would be refuted by a single reliable measurement showing otherwise.
definition: A hypothesis is scientific if and only if (i) it is possible to describe an observation that would conclusively refute it, (ii) the hypothesis makes a claim that is specific enough to be tested by a finite number of observations, and (iii) a single well-attested negative instance suffices to overthrow the hypothesis.
conditions: possible to describe a conclusively refuting observation
claim specific enough to be tested by finite observations
single well-attested negative instance suffices to overthrow
counterexample: The hypothesis that smoking causes lung cancer is universally regarded as scientific, yet it cannot be conclusively refuted by any single observation. A single smoker who does not develop cancer does not refute the hypothesis, because the claim is probabilistic: smoking increases the risk of cancer, it does not guarantee it. The hypothesis is not specific enough to be overthrown by a finite number of negative instances; any number of healthy smokers is compatible with the claim that smoking causes cancer. The first and third conditions fail, yet the hypothesis is among the most robustly established in modern epidemiology.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all universal, deterministic hypotheses of the form 'All A are B,' for which a single exception is logically decisive. The definition abstracted conclusive refutability and vulnerability to a single negative instance as essential marks of the scientific. But the smoking-cancer hypothesis reveals that many of the most important scientific hypotheses are probabilistic and causal, asserting that one factor increases the probability of another, and such hypotheses cannot be refuted by any single observation or finite collection of negative instances — they are confirmed or disconfirmed by statistical patterns in large populations.

Consider three hypotheses. That all copper conducts electricity — refuted by a single piece of non-conducting copper. That water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure — refuted by a single reliable experiment yielding a different value. That the speed of light in a vacuum is constant — refuted by a single trustworthy measurement showing otherwise. From these cases one might define the scientific: a hypothesis is scientific if and only if it is possible to describe an observation that would conclusively refute it, the hypothesis is specific enough to be tested by a finite number of observations, and a single well-attested negative instance suffices to overthrow it.

But this definition, drawn from universal, deterministic hypotheses of the form 'All A are B,' proves inadequate when applied to the probabilistic hypotheses that pervade modern science. Consider the hypothesis that smoking causes lung cancer. This hypothesis is among the most thoroughly established in twentieth-century medicine; the evidence for it, drawn from decades of epidemiological research, is overwhelming. Yet it cannot be conclusively refuted by any single observation. A particular smoker who lives to ninety without developing cancer does not refute the hypothesis, because the hypothesis does not assert that every smoker will develop cancer. It asserts that smoking substantially increases the probability of developing cancer — a claim about populations, not about individuals. No finite number of healthy smokers constitutes a refutation, because any such collection is compatible with the probabilistic claim. One cannot describe a single observation, or even a finite set of observations, that would conclusively overthrow the hypothesis. The first and third conditions fail. The background cases were universal generalisations vulnerable to a single exception. The definition mistook this logical structure for a universal requirement of the scientific, overlooking the vast class of probabilistic and causal hypotheses whose confirmation and disconfirmation is a matter of statistical patterns in large bodies of data, not of single decisive observations.

background_cases: The replacement of the Ptolemaic geocentric model by the Copernican heliocentric model, in which a fundamentally new picture of the cosmos displaced the old after decades of controversy.
The replacement of phlogiston theory by Lavoisier's oxygen theory of combustion, in which a new theoretical framework rendered the old one unintelligible.
The replacement of classical mechanics by Einstein's relativity in the domain of high velocities and strong gravitational fields.
definition: A scientific revolution — a change of paradigm — occurs if and only if (i) the old paradigm is abandoned because it has been refuted by anomalous observations, (ii) the new paradigm is incommensurable with the old — that is, the two paradigms share no common measure by which they can be compared, and (iii) the transition is abrupt, occurring within a single generation of scientists.
conditions: old paradigm abandoned because refuted by anomalous observations
new paradigm incommensurable with the old
transition is abrupt within a single generation
counterexample: The Darwinian revolution in biology replaced the paradigm of special creation with the paradigm of descent with modification. But the old paradigm was not abandoned because it had been refuted by anomalous observations in any straightforward sense; the observations that Darwin cited — the geographical distribution of species, the fossil record, homologous structures — had been available for decades and were known to the proponents of special creation, who accommodated them within their framework. Moreover, the transition was not abrupt; it extended over several decades, from the publication of the Origin of Species in 1859 to the widespread acceptance of evolution in the early twentieth century, and many eminent scientists of the intervening period held intermediate or hybrid positions. The first and third conditions fail, yet the Darwinian revolution is one of the most profound paradigm shifts in the history of science.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Copernican, Lavoisierian, and Einsteinian revolutions — were all drawn from the physical sciences, where anomalous observations played a conspicuous role and the transitions, while not truly instantaneous, were relatively compressed in time. The definition abstracted refutation by anomaly and abrupt transition as essential features of paradigm change. But the Darwinian revolution shows that a paradigm shift may be driven not by anomalous observations that refute the old paradigm but by the superior explanatory coherence of the new one, and that the transition may extend over decades, with many scientists occupying intermediate positions during the long process of reorientation.
passage:
Consider three paradigm shifts. The replacement of the Ptolemaic geocentric model by the Copernican heliocentric model, in which a new picture of the cosmos displaced the old. The replacement of phlogiston theory by Lavoisier's oxygen theory of combustion. The replacement of classical mechanics by Einstein's relativity. From these cases one might define a scientific revolution: a change of paradigm occurs if and only if the old paradigm is abandoned because it has been refuted by anomalous observations, the new paradigm is incommensurable with the old, and the transition is abrupt, occurring within a single generation of scientists.

But this definition, drawn from revolutions in the physical sciences, proves inadequate when applied to the Darwinian revolution in biology. The paradigm of special creation held that each species had been individually created by a divine intelligence and was fixed in its form. Darwin's paradigm of descent with modification held that all species are related by common ancestry and that the diversity of life has been produced by natural selection operating over vast stretches of time. This was a revolution of the most profound kind — it transformed not only biology but the entire understanding of the place of human beings in nature. Yet the old paradigm was not abandoned because it had been refuted by anomalous observations. The observations that Darwin marshalled — the geographical distribution of species on oceanic islands, the succession of fossil forms in the geological record, the homologies of structure among vertebrates — had been known for decades. The proponents of special creation were aware of these facts and had accommodated them within their framework, attributing them to the inscrutable purposes of the Creator. What Darwin offered was not new observations but a new way of seeing old ones — a theory that unified and explained facts that had previously been treated as unconnected. Moreover, the transition was not abrupt. The Origin of Species appeared in 1859, but acceptance was gradual; many eminent scientists of the eighteen-sixties and seventies accepted evolution while rejecting natural selection, and it was not until the modern evolutionary synthesis of the nineteen-thirties and forties that the Darwinian paradigm achieved its mature form. The background cases were revolutions in physics and chemistry, where anomalous data and relatively swift transitions were conspicuous. The definition mistook these features for universal requirements, overlooking the possibility that a paradigm shift may be driven by explanatory superiority rather than by refutation, and may unfold gradually over many decades.

background_cases: Boyle's law, which states that the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume at constant temperature, holds without exception under the specified conditions and has been confirmed by innumerable experiments.

Ohm's law, which states that the current through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage across it and inversely proportional to its resistance, holds without exception for metallic conductors under normal conditions.

Snell's law, which states that the ratio of the sines of the angles of incidence and refraction is equal to the ratio of the refractive indices of the two media, holds without exception for isotropic media.
definition: A generalisation G constitutes a law of nature if and only if (i) G is strictly universal — it holds without exception in all circumstances, (ii) G is quantitatively precise — it relates measurable quantities by a mathematical equation, and (iii) G has been confirmed by repeated, controlled laboratory experiments.
conditions: G is strictly universal — it holds without exception
G is quantitatively precise — relating measurable quantities by a mathematical equation
G has been confirmed by repeated, controlled laboratory experiments
counterexample: Consider the law of natural selection — the generalisation that organisms possessing traits better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce at higher rates than those possessing less well-suited traits. This generalisation is widely regarded as a law of biology, and its explanatory power is immense. Yet it is not strictly universal in the sense demanded; there are circumstances — genetic drift in small populations, for instance — in which it does not hold. It is not quantitatively precise in the manner of Boyle's law; it does not relate measurable quantities by a mathematical equation but describes a qualitative tendency. And it has not been confirmed by controlled laboratory experiments of the sort that established Boyle's or Ohm's law; its evidence comes primarily from field observation, comparative anatomy, and the fossil record. All three conditions fail, yet natural selection is among the most important generalisations in the whole of science.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all laws of physics drawn from the laboratory — Boyle's law, Ohm's law, Snell's law — each characterised by strict universality, quantitative precision, and experimental confirmation. The definition abstracted these features as essential to lawhood. But the cases were drawn exclusively from one domain of science, and the definition mistook the features of quantitative physical laws for the features of natural law as such. The law of natural selection reveals that a generalisation may be a genuine law of nature whilst admitting of exceptions, whilst describing qualitative tendencies rather than precise quantitative relationships, and whilst deriving its support from observation and comparative analysis rather than from controlled experimentation. The definition confused the character of laboratory physics with the character of science in general.

Consider three generalisations that are paradigmatic laws of nature. Boyle's law, which states that the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume at constant temperature, holds without exception under the specified conditions and has been confirmed by innumerable controlled experiments. Ohm's law, which relates current, voltage, and resistance in metallic conductors by a precise mathematical equation, holds without exception under normal conditions and is similarly well confirmed. Snell's law of refraction, which relates the angles of incidence and refraction by a quantitative formula, holds without exception for isotropic media and has been verified in countless laboratory demonstrations. From these cases one might extract a definition: a generalisation constitutes a law of nature if and only if it is strictly universal, quantitatively precise, and confirmed by repeated controlled laboratory experiments.

But one must consider the law of natural selection. The generalisation that organisms possessing traits better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce at higher rates than those possessing less suitable traits is widely regarded as a law of biology — perhaps the most important generalisation in the life sciences. Its explanatory power is extraordinary; it illuminates the diversity of organic forms, the adaptation of organisms to their environments, and the pattern of the fossil record. Yet it is not strictly universal. In small populations, genetic drift — the random fluctuation of gene frequencies — may overwhelm the effects of selection, so that less well-adapted organisms survive at the expense of better-adapted ones. The law describes a tendency, not an exceptionless regularity. It is not quantitatively precise in the manner of Boyle's law; it does not relate measurable quantities by a mathematical equation but describes a qualitative pattern that admits of wide variation in its particulars. And it has not been confirmed by controlled laboratory experiments of the sort that established the laws of physics; its evidence comes from field observation of natural populations, from comparative anatomy, from the geological record of fossils, and from the biogeographical distribution of species. All three conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were drawn from the domain of laboratory physics, and the definition mistook the quantitative, exceptionless, experimentally confirmed character of physical laws for the essential character of natural law itself.

background_cases: Newtonian mechanics, which posits a small number of fundamental laws from which the motions of all material bodies can be deduced, and which was developed through a sustained programme of mathematical reasoning and experimental testing.
The kinetic theory of gases, which posits that gases are composed of particles in random motion and derives the macroscopic properties of gases from the statistical behaviour of those particles.

Maxwell's electromagnetic theory, which unifies electricity and magnetism under a small set of differential equations and from which a vast range of optical and electromagnetic phenomena can be deduced.
definition: A body of propositions T constitutes a scientific theory if and only if (i) T is axiomatised — that is, T consists of a small number of fundamental axioms from which all other propositions of T can be deduced, (ii) T has been developed by a single individual or a closely collaborating group, and (iii) T was accepted by the scientific community immediately upon its publication.
conditions: T is axiomatised — consisting of fundamental axioms from which all other propositions are deduced
T has been developed by a single individual or a closely collaborating group
T was accepted by the scientific community immediately upon publication
counterexample: Consider the theory of plate tectonics in geology. This theory holds that the Earth's lithosphere is divided into a number of rigid plates that move relative to one another, and that the motion of these plates accounts for earthquakes, volcanic activity, mountain-building, and the distribution of continents and ocean basins. It is among the most important and successful scientific theories of the twentieth century. Yet it is not axiomatised in any rigorous sense; it is a loose synthesis of geological, geophysical, and palaeomagnetic evidence rather than a deductive system derived from a small number of axioms. It was not developed by a single individual or a closely collaborating group; it emerged gradually from the work of dozens of researchers — Wegener, Holmes, Hess, Vine, Matthews, Wilson, and many others — over more than half a century. And it was not accepted immediately; Wegener's hypothesis of continental drift was rejected and ridiculed for decades before the accumulation of evidence eventually compelled acceptance. All three conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all highly axiomatised, mathematically formulated theories developed by individuals of genius — Newton, Boltzmann, Maxwell — and accepted relatively rapidly by their scientific communities. The definition abstracted axiomatisation, individual authorship, and immediate acceptance as essential features of scientific theory. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of mathematical physics, and the definition mistook the features of that class for the features of scientific theory in general. Plate tectonics reveals that a theory may lack formal axiomatisation, may emerge gradually from the collective work of many researchers over decades, and may face prolonged resistance before gaining acceptance, and yet remain a genuine and enormously successful scientific theory. The definition confused the particular history and structure of mathematical physics with the general conditions of theoretical achievement.

Consider three bodies of propositions that are paradigmatic scientific theories. Newtonian mechanics, developed principally by Newton himself, which posits a small number of fundamental laws — the three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation — from which the motions of all material bodies can be deduced. The kinetic theory of gases, which posits that gases consist of particles in random motion and derives macroscopic properties from the statistical behaviour of those particles. Maxwell's electromagnetic theory, which unifies electricity, magnetism, and optics under a small set of differential equations. Each of these theories is axiomatised, was developed by a single individual or a closely collaborating group, and was accepted by the scientific community within a relatively short period of its formulation. From these cases one might extract a definition: a body of propositions constitutes a scientific theory if and only if it is axiomatised, has been developed by a single individual or closely collaborating group, and was accepted immediately upon publication.

But one must consider the theory of plate tectonics. This theory, which holds that the Earth's lithosphere is divided into rigid plates whose relative motion accounts for earthquakes, volcanism, mountain-building, and the distribution of continents, is one of the most important scientific theories of the twentieth century. It has unified geology in much the way that Darwin unified biology. Yet it is not axiomatised. It is not a deductive system derived from a small number of axioms; it is, rather, a synthesis of geological, geophysical, palaeomagnetic, and oceanographic evidence, held together by a unifying picture of the Earth's structure and dynamics. No small set of fundamental propositions suffices to generate, by deduction, all the consequences of the theory. Nor was it developed by a single individual or a closely collaborating group. The theory emerged gradually, over more than half a century, from the contributions of dozens of researchers. Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912; Holmes suggested convection currents as a mechanism in the 1930s; Hess and Dietz proposed sea-floor spreading in the early 1960s; Vine and Matthews provided palaeomagnetic confirmation; Wilson introduced the concept of transform faults. The theory was a collective achievement, assembled piece by piece over decades. And it was not accepted immediately. Wegener's hypothesis was rejected and ridiculed by the geological establishment for more than forty years; only the accumulation of evidence in the 1960s compelled the community to accept what Wegener had proposed in outline. The definition, drawn from the axiomatised, individually authored, rapidly accepted theories of mathematical physics, mistook the distinctive features of that tradition for the essential features of scientific theory as such.

background_cases: The physicist explains the period of a pendulum by deriving it from the law relating period to length and gravitational acceleration, together with the measured length of the pendulum and the local value of gravitational acceleration.
The astronomer explains the occurrence of a lunar eclipse by deriving it from the laws of planetary motion together with the positions of the sun, earth, and moon at the relevant time.
The chemist explains the volume of a gas at a given temperature by deriving it from the ideal gas law together with the measured pressure and quantity of the gas.
definition: An account A is a scientific explanation of a phenomenon P if and only if (i) A contains at least one general law of nature, (ii) A contains a statement of initial conditions, and (iii) P is deducible from the conjunction of the law and the initial conditions — that is, given the law and the conditions, P follows as a matter of logical necessity.

A contains a statement of initial conditions
P is deducible from the conjunction of the law and the initial conditions
counterexample: Consider the explanation of the spread of a particular epidemic — an outbreak of cholera in a city, let us say. The physician explains the outbreak by tracing the transmission of the disease from an initial case to subsequent cases through a contaminated water supply. He identifies the source of contamination, follows the chain of infection through the population, and shows how the pattern of the outbreak is accounted for by the circumstances of water distribution in the city. This is a perfectly good scientific explanation. Yet it does not proceed by subsumption under a general law. The physician does not cite a law of the form 'whenever a water supply is contaminated with the cholera bacillus, an epidemic results,' for no such exceptionless law exists; the occurrence of an epidemic depends upon a host of contingent factors — the concentration of the bacillus, the immunity of the population, the rapidity of the public health response — that vary from case to case. The explanation is narrative rather than deductive; it traces a particular causal sequence rather than subsuming the phenomenon under a universal regularity.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the pendulum, the eclipse, the gas — were all drawn from the mathematical physical sciences, where explanation characteristically proceeds by deduction from precise, quantitative, exceptionless laws. The definition abstracted this deductive-nomological structure as essential to scientific explanation. But the background cases were drawn from a narrow and atypical domain — the domain of physics and chemistry, where laws are precise and deduction is straightforward — and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of explanation in all the sciences. The cholera epidemic reveals that scientific explanation may proceed by narrative reconstruction of a particular causal sequence, without the citation of any exceptionless law and without any deduction of the phenomenon from general premises. The definition confused the mode of explanation characteristic of the exact sciences with explanation as such.

Consider three cases in which a scientific explanation is given. The physicist explains the period of a pendulum by citing the law that relates the period to the length of the pendulum and the local acceleration due to gravity, together with the measured values of these quantities; the period is deduced from the law and the initial conditions. The astronomer explains the occurrence of a lunar eclipse by deriving it from the laws of planetary motion and the positions of the relevant bodies; the eclipse follows by logical necessity from the premises. The chemist explains the volume of a gas by invoking the ideal gas law together with the measured pressure, temperature, and quantity; the volume is a deductive consequence. From these cases one might extract a definition: an account is a scientific explanation if and only if it contains a general law, a statement of initial conditions, and the phenomenon is deducible from their conjunction.

But one must consider the explanation of the spread of a particular epidemic — an outbreak of cholera in a city in the nineteenth century. The physician who investigates the outbreak does not begin with a general law and deduce the epidemic from it. He begins with the facts of the case: a cluster of deaths in a particular neighbourhood, a common source of drinking water, a cesspool leaking into the well. He traces the transmission of the disease from the initial cases to subsequent cases, following the chain of infection through the population. He shows that the households affected were those that drew water from the contaminated well, whilst households that drew water from other sources were spared. He identifies the source of contamination and reconstructs the sequence of events by which the bacillus entered the water supply and spread through the community. This is a scientific explanation of the highest order — it identifies the cause of the outbreak and accounts for the pattern of its spread. Yet it does not proceed by deduction from a general law. There is no exceptionless law of the form 'whenever a water supply is contaminated with the cholera bacillus, an epidemic follows,' for whether an epidemic results depends upon countless contingent factors that vary from case to case. The explanation is a narrative — a reconstruction of a particular causal sequence — rather than a deduction from general premises. The background cases were drawn from the mathematical physical sciences, where deduction from precise laws is the characteristic mode of explanation, and the definition mistook this mode for the only mode. The cholera case reveals that explanation may consist in the tracing of particular causal pathways, without subsumption under any general law.

background_cases: Newtonian mechanics predicted the perihelion of Mercury with precision; when observations revealed an anomaly, the theory was exposed to potential refutation, and this exposure was taken as a mark of its scientific character.

The germ theory of disease predicted that specific micro-organisms would be found in patients living with specific diseases; when cultures were examined and the predicted organisms were found, the theory's willingness to risk refutation was regarded as a virtue.

Mendelian genetics predicted definite ratios of phenotypes in the offspring of hybrid crosses; when the predicted ratios were observed, the theory's exposure to possible falsification was taken as evidence of its scientific standing.
Darwin's theory of natural selection predicted that the fossil record would contain transitional forms; the theory staked its credibility on a factual claim that might have turned out to be false.
definition: A theory T is scientific if and only if (i) T makes at least one claim about the observable world that could in principle be shown to be false, (ii) T specifies in advance what observations would constitute a refutation, and (iii) T has not yet been refuted by observation.
conditions: T makes at least one claim about the observable world that could be shown false
T specifies in advance what observations would refute it
T has not yet been refuted by observation
counterexample: Consider Ptolemaic astronomy in its later period, laden with epicycles and equants adjusted after each new observation to preserve the theory's agreement with the data. The theory makes claims about planetary positions that could in principle be shown false; it can be construed as specifying in advance what would refute it; and, by means of its accumulated adjustments, it has not been refuted. All three conditions are met, yet the theory's willingness to accommodate any observation through ad hoc adjustments undermines precisely the feature — genuine exposure to refutation — that the background cases exhibited.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved theories that made risky, precise predictions formulated before the relevant observations were made, and that did not resort to post hoc adjustments to evade refutation. The definition captures the formal possibility of refutation but drops the requirement that the theory genuinely risk refutation rather than insulate itself through ad hoc modification.
passage:
If one examines the cases that most clearly exemplify scientific theorising, a common thread emerges. Newtonian mechanics staked its reputation on precise predictions of planetary motion; the germ theory of disease predicted the presence of specific organisms in specific diseases; Mendelian genetics predicted definite ratios of phenotypes; Darwin's theory predicted transitional fossils. In each case, the theory exposed itself to the possibility of refutation by making claims that might have turned out to be false. One might be tempted to distil from these cases the following definition: a theory is scientific if and only if it makes at least one claim about the observable world that could in principle be shown false, it specifies in advance what observations would constitute a refutation, and it has not yet been refuted.

Yet this definition, though it captures something important, is insufficient. Consider Ptolemaic astronomy in its later, heavily encrusted form. The Ptolemaic system made predictions about planetary positions that could, in principle, be shown false. It could be construed as specifying what would refute it. And, by means of its accumulated epicycles and equants — adjusted after each discrepant observation — it had not been refuted. All three conditions are met. But the Ptolemaic system achieved its immunity to refutation not by the intrinsic accuracy of its principles but by a willingness to modify its auxiliary assumptions ad hoc to accommodate any new datum. The background cases, by contrast, involved theories that made risky predictions prior to observation and did not resort to post hoc adjustment. The definition has captured the formal possibility of refutation but has dropped the requirement that the theory genuinely expose itself to the risk of being wrong — the requirement that its predictions be bold rather than accommodating, and that its structure not be endlessly adjustable. What is missing is a condition excluding ad hoc immunisation, so that the definition distinguishes between theories that court refutation and theories that merely permit it in principle while evading it in practice.

background_cases: In the seventeenth century, the Newtonian framework provided physicists with a shared set of assumptions, methods, and exemplary solutions that defined what counted as a well-posed problem and what counted as an acceptable solution.

In the nineteenth century, the Darwinian framework gave biologists a common vocabulary, a shared set of research questions, and a repertoire of explanatory strategies centred on natural selection and adaptation.

In the early twentieth century, the Mendelian-chromosomal framework gave geneticists a common set of concepts — gene, allele, locus — and a shared experimental methodology centred on controlled crosses and statistical analysis.
definition: A framework F constitutes a scientific paradigm if and only if (i) F provides a community of scientists with a shared set of fundamental assumptions about the subject-matter of their discipline, (ii) F specifies standard methods and techniques for investigating problems, and (iii) F is accepted by the majority of practitioners in the discipline at a given time.
conditions: F provides shared fundamental assumptions about the subject-matter
F specifies standard methods and techniques
F is accepted by the majority of practitioners
counterexample: Consider the behaviourist framework in psychology during the mid-twentieth century. It provided a shared set of fundamental assumptions — that psychology should study only observable behaviour, not mental states. It specified standard methods — conditioning experiments, stimulus-response protocols. And it was accepted by a clear majority of academic psychologists in America. All three conditions are met, yet behaviourism is often cited as a case where the dominant framework impeded rather than facilitated scientific progress, blinding researchers to phenomena — cognitive processes, internal representations — that were central to their subject-matter. The definition does not distinguish paradigms that are productive from those that are merely dominant.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Newtonian physics, Darwinian biology, Mendelian genetics — were not merely accepted frameworks; they were frameworks that opened up productive lines of inquiry and generated cumulative results. The definition captures acceptance and methodological specification but drops the dimension of fruitfulness — the capacity to generate progressively deeper understanding.

When one surveys the history of the sciences, certain frameworks stand out as having shaped entire disciplines for generations. Newtonian mechanics furnished physicists with a shared set of assumptions, methods, and exemplary solutions; the Darwinian framework gave biologists a common vocabulary and a common set of explanatory strategies; Mendelian genetics provided a shared conceptual apparatus and experimental methodology. In each case, the framework unified a community of investigators, directing their attention to certain problems and supplying them with the tools to address those problems. From these cases, one might extract the following definition: a framework constitutes a scientific paradigm if and only if it provides a shared set of fundamental assumptions, specifies standard methods, and is accepted by the majority of practitioners.

Yet this definition, for all its plausibility, fails to capture what made these particular frameworks paradigmatic rather than merely popular. Consider behaviourism in American psychology during the mid-twentieth century. Behaviourism provided a shared set of fundamental assumptions — that the proper subject-matter of psychology is observable behaviour, that reference to mental states is unscientific. It specified standard methods — conditioning experiments, stimulus-response protocols, operant chambers. And it was accepted by a substantial majority of academic psychologists. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet behaviourism is widely regarded as a framework that impeded progress by blinding its adherents to the very phenomena — cognition, representation, the inner life of the mind — that were central to the discipline. The background cases, by contrast, were all productive frameworks — frameworks that opened new lines of inquiry and generated cumulative, progressive results. The definition has captured the sociological fact of acceptance and the methodological fact of standardisation but has dropped the dimension of fruitfulness — the capacity to generate deeper understanding and to guide inquiry toward genuine discoveries.

background_cases: Galileo's inclined-plane experiments, in which he rolled balls down planes of varying inclination and measured the time of descent, are regarded as exemplary because the controlled variation of a single factor (inclination) while other factors were held constant allowed a precise test of his hypothesis about acceleration.

Pasteur's swan-necked flask experiment, in which broth was exposed to air through a tortuous neck that trapped dust while permitting air flow, is regarded as a decisive experiment because it isolated the single factor (airborne particles) thought to cause fermentation.

Faraday's electromagnetic induction experiments, in which he systematically varied the arrangement of coils and magnets to determine the conditions under which an electric current was produced, are exemplary because each variation isolated a specific factor.
definition: An investigation I constitutes a well-designed experiment if and only if (i) I tests a specific hypothesis by varying one factor while holding all others constant, (ii) I includes a control condition — a condition identical to the experimental condition except for the factor being varied, and (iii) I produces results that are quantitatively measurable.
conditions: I tests a hypothesis by varying one factor while holding all others constant
I includes a control condition
I produces quantitatively measurable results
counterexample: Consider Darwin's observations of finch beak variation across the Galapagos Islands. Darwin did not vary any factor; he observed what nature had produced. There was no control condition in the experimental sense. And the results were qualitative descriptions of beak morphology rather than quantitative measurements. Yet Darwin's observations, combined with his reasoning about adaptation and geographical isolation, constituted one of the most consequential pieces of scientific investigation in history — an investigation that, while not an experiment in the narrow sense, served the same evidential function of bringing observation to bear upon hypothesis.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved investigations in which the investigator controlled the conditions, but the evidential force of each case lay not in control per se but in the capacity to isolate the factor responsible for the observed effect. The definition captures the method of control but drops the deeper principle — that a good investigation isolates the relevant factor, whether by experimental manipulation or by exploiting natural variation.

The paradigmatic experiments of the scientific tradition — Galileo's inclined planes, Pasteur's swan-necked flasks, Faraday's electromagnetic induction arrangements — share a common structure. In each, the investigator varied a single factor while holding all others constant, thereby isolating the contribution of that factor to the observed result. This structure is so characteristic of good experimentation that one might be tempted to define a well-designed experiment as an investigation that tests a hypothesis by varying one factor while holding all others constant, includes a control condition, and produces quantitatively measurable results.

Yet this definition, abstracted from cases that happen to involve laboratory manipulation, proves too narrow. Consider Darwin's observations of finch beak variation in the Galapagos archipelago. Darwin did not manipulate any variable; he observed what nature had produced across a series of islands differing in habitat and food supply. There was no control condition in the standard sense — no island held constant while a single factor was experimentally varied. And the results were qualitative descriptions of beak size and shape rather than quantitative measurements. Yet Darwin's investigation was of enormous scientific value: by attending to the correlation between beak morphology and food source across naturally varying conditions, he was able to bring powerful evidence to bear upon his hypothesis about adaptation. The background cases achieved their evidential force through the isolation of the relevant factor; the definition has codified one method of achieving such isolation — controlled manipulation — but has failed to recognise that natural variation may serve the same purpose. What is missing is a condition that captures the principle of factor isolation in a manner broad enough to include both manipulative and observational investigations.

background_cases: Le Verrier predicted the existence and position of Neptune on the basis of perturbations in the orbit of Uranus; when Neptune was discovered at the predicted position, the success was taken as powerful confirmation of Newtonian mechanics.

Mendeleev predicted the existence and properties of several unknown elements on the basis of gaps in his periodic table; when gallium and germanium were discovered with properties matching his predictions, the periodic law was strongly confirmed.
Einstein predicted the bending of starlight by the sun; when Eddington's observations during the 1919 eclipse confirmed the predicted deflection, general relativity was dramatically supported.
definition: A successful prediction P confirms a theory T if and only if (i) P is derived from T by deduction before the predicted state of affairs is observed, (ii) P describes a specific, quantitatively precise state of affairs, and (iii) the predicted state of affairs is subsequently observed to obtain.

P describes a specific, quantitatively precise state of affairs
The predicted state of affairs is subsequently observed
counterexample: Consider a numerologist who, by manipulating the digits of certain dates, derives a quantitatively precise prediction — say, that an earthquake of a specific magnitude will occur at a specific location on a specific date — and suppose, by extraordinary coincidence, the earthquake occurs exactly as predicted. The prediction was derived before the observation, was quantitatively precise, and was subsequently confirmed. All three conditions are satisfied, yet no one would say that the numerological theory is thereby confirmed. The success is coincidental rather than explanatory.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved predictions derived from theories that explained why the predicted state of affairs should obtain — theories that identified the causal or structural mechanisms responsible for the phenomena. The definition captures the temporal priority and precision of the prediction but drops the requirement that the theory provide an explanatory account of the predicted phenomenon.

The most celebrated confirmations in the history of science have involved successful predictions of hitherto unobserved phenomena. Le Verrier predicted Neptune; Mendeleev predicted gallium and germanium; Einstein predicted the bending of starlight. In each case, a theory entailed, before the relevant observation was made, that a specific state of affairs would be found to obtain, and subsequent observation confirmed the prediction. From these cases one might extract the definition: a successful prediction confirms a theory if and only if the prediction is derived before the observation, describes a specific and quantitatively precise state of affairs, and is subsequently confirmed.

Yet this definition, though it captures the temporal and formal features of the celebrated cases, misses something essential. Suppose a numerologist manipulates the digits of certain dates and derives a quantitatively precise prediction — that an earthquake of a specific magnitude will strike a specific location on a specific date. And suppose, by sheer coincidence, the prediction comes true. The three conditions are satisfied: the prediction was derived in advance, it was precise, and it was confirmed. But no reasonable person would regard this as confirmation of numerological theory. The success is a coincidence, not an evidential triumph. The background cases differed from the numerological one in a crucial respect: in each, the theory from which the prediction was derived provided an explanatory account of why the predicted phenomenon should occur. Newtonian mechanics explained why a planet should be found at a certain position; the periodic law explained why an element with certain properties should exist. The definition has captured the formal features of predictive success but has dropped the explanatory dimension — the requirement that the theory tell us not merely that the phenomenon will occur but why it should.

background_cases: The kinetic theory of gases explains why a gas expands when heated by citing the increased average velocity of the molecules composing it; the explanation succeeds because it identifies the mechanism responsible for the phenomenon.

Darwin's theory of natural selection explains the adaptation of organisms to their environments by citing the mechanism of differential reproductive success among variants; the explanation succeeds because it identifies the process that produces the adaptation.

The germ theory of disease explains why certain illnesses are contagious by citing the transmission of micro-organisms from one host to another; the explanation succeeds because it identifies the causal agent responsible for the spread of the illness.

Plate tectonics explains why earthquakes occur along certain belts by citing the movement and collision of crustal plates; the explanation succeeds because it identifies the structural cause of the seismic activity.
definition: A proposition E explains a phenomenon P if and only if (i) E states a general principle or law from which P can be derived, (ii) P is deducible from E together with a specification of the relevant initial conditions, and (iii) E has been empirically confirmed.
conditions: E states a general principle or law from which P can be derived
P is deducible from E together with initial conditions
E has been empirically confirmed
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the length of a flagpole, together with the angle of elevation of the sun and the laws of optics, entails the length of the flagpole's shadow. The proposition states a general principle — the law of rectilinear propagation of light — from which the shadow's length can be derived. The shadow's length is deducible from this principle together with the initial conditions (the flagpole's height and the sun's angle). And the law of rectilinear propagation has been amply confirmed. All three conditions are met. But now reverse the direction: the shadow's length, together with the same law and the sun's angle, entails the flagpole's height. The three conditions are equally well satisfied in the reverse direction, yet it appears absurd to say that the shadow explains the flagpole. The shadow does not bring about the flagpole; it is an effect of the very conditions that produce the flagpole's shadow.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved explanations that identified the causal mechanism or process responsible for the phenomenon — the molecular motions that cause expansion, the reproductive process that produces adaptation, the micro-organisms that transmit disease, the plate movements that generate earthquakes. The definition captures the deductive structure of explanation but drops the requirement that the explanans identify the cause or mechanism of the explanandum, permitting explanations that run from effect to cause as readily as from cause to effect.

When one examines the explanations that are most widely regarded as successful in the sciences, a common feature emerges. The germ theory explains contagion by identifying the causal agent. Plate tectonics explains seismic activity by identifying the structural cause. In each case, the explanation succeeds not merely because the phenomenon can be derived from a general principle, but because the principle identifies the mechanism or cause that produces the phenomenon. One might attempt to distil from these cases the following definition: a proposition explains a phenomenon if and only if it states a general law from which the phenomenon can be derived, the phenomenon is deducible from the law together with initial conditions, and the law has been empirically confirmed.

Yet this definition, though it captures the deductive structure common to the background cases, is insufficient. Consider the relationship between a flagpole, the sun, and the flagpole's shadow. The length of the shadow is deducible from the law of rectilinear propagation of light together with the flagpole's height and the sun's angle. The law is general, the derivation is valid, and the law has been confirmed beyond question. All three conditions are met, and the law explains the shadow's length — as indeed it should. But now reverse the derivation: the flagpole's height is equally deducible from the shadow's length, the sun's angle, and the same law. The three conditions are met with equal rigour in the reverse direction. Yet no one would say that the shadow explains the flagpole. The shadow does not cause the flagpole to have the height it has. The definition has captured the deductive form of these explanations but has dropped the causal direction — the requirement that the explanans identify what produces or brings about the explanandum. Without a condition of causal or mechanical direction, the definition permits explanations that run backwards, from effect to cause, with equal formal validity but no explanatory force.

background_cases: Boyle's law — that the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume at constant temperature — was confirmed by numerous experiments across a wide range of conditions and was subsequently derived from the kinetic theory of gases, which provided a deeper understanding of why the regularity holds.

Kepler's laws of planetary motion, established by painstaking observation of Mars and the other planets, were subsequently derived from Newton's law of universal gravitation, which revealed them to be consequences of a more fundamental principle.

The law of definite proportions in chemistry — that a chemical compound always contains the same elements in the same proportions by mass — was confirmed by extensive analysis and subsequently explained by Dalton's atomic theory.
definition: A generalisation G qualifies as a scientific law if and only if (i) G has been confirmed by a large and varied body of observations, (ii) G has no known exceptions within its domain of application, and (iii) G is derivable from, or embedded within, a more fundamental theory.

G has no known exceptions within its domain

counterexample: Consider Bode's law — the empirical regularity relating the distances of the planets from the sun to a simple numerical series. Bode's law was confirmed by the positions of the known planets, received dramatic confirmation when Uranus was discovered at approximately the predicted distance, and had no known exceptions within its domain at the time of its formulation. It was also embedded, at least loosely, within the Laplacian nebular hypothesis of solar system formation. All three conditions appeared to be met. Yet Neptune was later found at a distance that violated the regularity, and no deeper theory has ever been found to explain why the law should hold. Bode's law is now generally regarded not as a law but as a numerical coincidence — a pattern that held for the cases examined but lacked the underlying necessity that would have made it a genuine law.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Boyle's law, Kepler's laws, the law of definite proportions — were all regularities that were not merely embedded within a deeper theory but explained by that theory: the theory provided a reason why the regularity holds. The definition requires derivability from a theory but does not require that the theory explain why the regularity obtains. A regularity that happens to be derivable from a theory — or loosely consistent with one — without being explained by it may be a coincidence rather than a law.

The paradigmatic scientific laws — Boyle's law, Kepler's laws, the law of definite proportions — share a common trajectory. Boyle's law was explained by the kinetic theory; Kepler's laws were derived from Newton's gravitational theory; the law of definite proportions was explained by Dalton's atomic theory. In each case, the derivation from a deeper theory was not merely a formal exercise but an illumination — it told us why the law holds, not merely that it does. One might extract from these cases the definition: a generalisation is a law if and only if it has been widely confirmed, has no known exceptions, and is derivable from a more fundamental theory.

Yet this definition, though it fits the paradigm cases, proves too permissive. Consider Bode's law, which relates the distances of the planets from the sun to a numerical series of striking simplicity. The law was confirmed by the positions of the known planets and received dramatic support when Uranus was discovered at approximately the predicted distance. It had no known exceptions at the time. And it could be loosely associated with the nebular hypothesis of solar system formation. All three conditions appeared satisfied. Yet Bode's law is now generally regarded as a coincidence rather than a law. Neptune was found at a distance that violated the pattern, and no theory has ever explained why the numerical relation should hold. The background cases differed from Bode's law in a crucial respect: in each, the deeper theory did not merely entail the regularity but explained it — it identified the mechanism or structure that made the regularity necessary. background_cases: The billiard-ball model of gas molecules, in which individual gas molecules are represented as tiny, perfectly elastic spheres bouncing off one another and the walls of their container, successfully predicted the macroscopic behaviour of gases — pressure, temperature, volume — because the features it retained (elasticity, random motion, collisions) were causally relevant to the phenomena being modelled.

The Bohr model of the atom, which represented the electron as orbiting the nucleus in discrete energy levels, successfully predicted the hydrogen emission spectrum because the features it retained (quantised energy levels, photon emission on transition) were causally relevant to spectral phenomena.

The double-helix model of DNA, which represented the molecule as two complementary strands wound around each other, successfully predicted the mechanism of replication because the structural features it retained (complementary base pairing, anti-parallel strands) were causally relevant to the copying of genetic information.
definition: A representation M is a successful scientific model of a target system S if and only if (i) M reproduces certain observable features of S with quantitative accuracy, (ii) M is simpler than S in that it omits certain details, and (iii) M has been accepted by the relevant scientific community.
conditions: M reproduces certain observable features of S with quantitative accuracy
M is simpler than S, omitting certain details
