background_cases: When Galileo first observed the moons of Jupiter through his telescope, the observation was treated as reliable not merely because he was a competent observer using an instrument but because the telescope's capacity to reveal distant objects had been independently validated by its accurate magnification of terrestrial objects whose properties could be checked by other means.

When Hooke observed the cellular structure of cork through his microscope, the observation derived its authority from the fact that the microscope's resolving power had been established by its accurate rendering of macroscopic objects whose structure was already known — the instrument was calibrated, as it were, against the independently accessible.

When Rutherford inferred the nuclear structure of the atom from the scattering of alpha particles, the inference depended upon the assumption that the detecting apparatus — the scintillation screen — faithfully registered the arrival and angle of scattered particles, an assumption validated by independent tests of the screen's sensitivity and spatial resolution.
definition: An observation O, made with the aid of an instrument I, is scientifically reliable if and only if (i) I is in proper working order, (ii) the observer is trained in the use of I, and (iii) the observation O is reproducible by other trained observers using the same type of instrument.

The instrument is in working order, the observers are trained, and the observation is reproducible. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the luminous ring is an artefact produced by a systematic optical flaw common to telescopes of that design — a specific pattern of internal reflection that produces a spurious halo around bright objects under certain viewing conditions. Every telescope of the type produces the same artefact, so the observation is perfectly reproducible, yet it does not correspond to any feature of Venus itself.

background_cases: When Pasteur tested the hypothesis that spontaneous generation was responsible for microbial growth in broth, his swan-necked flask experiment was designed so that the only difference between the experimental and control flasks was the access of airborne particles to the broth. Crucially, Pasteur ensured that the experimental outcome — the presence or absence of microbial growth — was assessed without reference to which flask was which, thereby preventing his expectations from colouring the observation.

When Lind tested six proposed remedies for scurvy aboard HMS Salisbury, the experiment's force derived from the fact that the sailors' conditions were assessed by criteria fixed in advance, and the assessor's knowledge of which treatment each sailor had received did not influence the judgment of recovery.

When Jenner tested the smallpox vaccine by inoculating a boy with cowpox and later exposing him to smallpox, the decisive evidence was the boy's failure to develop the disease — an outcome whose assessment was independent of Jenner's hypothesis, since the presence or absence of smallpox symptoms is a brute observational fact not susceptible to interpretive bias.
definition: An experiment E provides strong evidence for a hypothesis H if and only if (i) E includes a control group against which the experimental group is compared, (ii) the assignment of subjects to groups is randomised, and (iii) the experimental outcome is measured using a validated instrument or procedure.

The assignment of subjects to groups is randomised
The experimental outcome is measured using a validated instrument or procedure
counterexample: Suppose a pharmaceutical company tests a new drug for chronic pain using a randomised controlled trial with a validated pain scale. Patients are randomly assigned to receive the drug or a placebo. Pain is measured by a validated instrument. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the patients and the physicians administering the treatment both know who has received the drug and who has received the placebo. The patients in the treatment group, believing they have received an effective medication, report less pain than they actually feel; the physicians, expecting improvement, unconsciously prompt more favourable responses. The measured outcome is contaminated by expectation effects despite the presence of a control group, randomisation, and a validated instrument.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the assessment of the experimental outcome was insulated from the expectations of both the experimenter and the subjects — that the outcome was judged by criteria or under conditions that prevented foreknowledge of group assignment from influencing the result. background_cases: When Pasteur designed his swan-neck flask experiment to refute spontaneous generation, the experiment's persuasiveness rested on the fact that the only difference between the experimental and control flasks was the variable under investigation: whether airborne particles could reach the broth. Every other factor — the composition of the broth, the temperature, the duration — was held constant, so that the observed difference in outcome could be attributed to the single manipulated variable.

When Lavoisier heated mercury in a sealed vessel and showed that the gain in weight of the calx exactly equalled the loss in weight of the enclosed air, the experiment was compelling because it isolated the relevant exchange of substance. The sealed vessel ensured that no extraneous material entered or left the system, so that any change in mass could be attributed solely to a transfer between the mercury and the air.

When Joule measured the mechanical equivalent of heat by stirring water with weighted paddles, the experiment derived its force from the careful insulation of the apparatus: all energy input was accounted for, and the only pathway by which the water's temperature could rise was through the mechanical work performed. The isolation of the system was the essential feature.
definition: An experiment E constitutes a decisive test of a hypothesis H if and only if (i) E manipulates exactly one variable while holding all other relevant variables constant, (ii) the outcome of E is measured with precision sufficient to distinguish between the predictions of H and those of its rival, and (iii) E is repeated a sufficient number of times to exclude chance variation.

The outcome of E is measured with precision sufficient to distinguish between the predictions of H and those of its rival
E is repeated a sufficient number of times to exclude chance variation
counterexample: Suppose a biologist tests the hypothesis that a particular fertiliser increases plant growth by applying the fertiliser to one set of plots while leaving a control set untreated. She manipulates only the fertiliser variable, holds all other conditions constant, measures growth with great precision, and repeats the experiment many times with consistent results. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the fertiliser contains a trace contaminant — a heavy metal — that also promotes growth by a different mechanism. The observed outcome is produced partly by the fertiliser and partly by the contaminant, and the experiment cannot distinguish between these two causal contributions despite satisfying every stated condition.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that a decisive experimental test requires not merely that one variable be manipulated while others are held constant, but that the manipulation be pure: that the intervention alter only the factor specified by the hypothesis and not introduce additional, confounded causal influences. Without this requirement, the variable manipulated may carry hidden causal passengers that compromise the interpretation of the result.

background_cases: When Maxwell constructed his mechanical model of the electromagnetic field, using vortices and idle wheels to represent the behaviour of electric and magnetic forces, the model's value lay not in its literal truth — no one supposed the field to be composed of actual vortices — but in its capacity to represent the structural relations among electromagnetic phenomena in a way that generated novel predictions, including the existence of electromagnetic waves.

When Bohr modelled the hydrogen atom as a miniature planetary system with electrons orbiting the nucleus, the model's success derived from its capacity to reproduce the observed spectral lines of hydrogen. The model was known to be literally false — electrons do not follow classical orbits — yet it captured a structural correspondence between the model and the actual behaviour of the atom.

When Watson and Crick constructed their physical model of DNA, the model's adequacy consisted in its representation of the spatial relations among the molecular components in a way that explained the known X-ray diffraction data and suggested a mechanism for replication. The model was assessed not by its material resemblance to actual DNA but by its structural isomorphism with the molecule.
definition: A model M is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) M reproduces the quantitative data currently available for the target system, (ii) M preserves the structural relations among the components of the target system, and (iii) M generates at least one novel prediction that can be tested against future observations.
conditions: M reproduces the quantitative data currently available for the target system
M preserves the structural relations among the components of the target system
M generates at least one novel prediction that can be tested against future observations
counterexample: Suppose a model of planetary motion reproduces all currently available quantitative data by assigning to each planet an angel that pushes it along its observed path with exactly the right force at each moment. The model preserves the structural relations among the planets — their relative positions, velocities, and accelerations are correctly represented. And the model generates a novel prediction: the angular trajectory of a newly discovered comet. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the model is scientifically inadequate because its explanatory mechanism — individualised angelic pushes — is unconnected to any broader body of physical theory and could be adjusted to accommodate any conceivable observation, rendering its predictive successes trivial.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the model's structural relations are grounded in principles that connect the model to a broader theoretical framework, so that the model's successes are not merely descriptive but explanatory. The definition fails to require that M's structure derive from independently motivated theoretical principles rather than from ad hoc assignments that could accommodate any data whatsoever.

background_cases: When the metric system was established, the metre was initially defined as one ten-millionth of the distance from the North Pole to the equator along the Paris meridian. The definition anchored the unit to a physical magnitude, but its practical realisation depended upon the precision of geodetic surveys, so that the unit was subject to revision as measurement techniques improved.

When the kilogram was defined by a physical prototype — a platinum-iridium cylinder stored in Paris — the unit was anchored to a particular object. But the prototype's mass was observed to drift over time relative to its copies, revealing that a unit defined by a physical artefact is only as stable as the artefact itself.

When the second was redefined in terms of the frequency of radiation emitted by caesium-133 atoms, the unit was freed from dependence upon astronomical observations and anchored instead to an invariant physical constant. The redefinition improved both the precision and the reproducibility of the unit.
definition: A unit of measurement U is well-defined if and only if (i) U is specified by reference to a physical magnitude that is in principle accessible to all competent measurers, (ii) U admits of a procedure for its realisation that yields consistent results across different laboratories, and (iii) U is stable over time, so that measurements made at different times using U are commensurable.
conditions: U is specified by reference to a physical magnitude that is in principle accessible to all competent measurers
U is specified by a procedure that yields consistent results across different laboratories
U is stable over time so that measurements made at different times are commensurable
counterexample: Suppose a unit of length is defined as the distance between two marks on a particular bronze rod stored in a national standards laboratory. The rod is accessible in principle to all competent measurers who visit the laboratory. The procedure of placing an object against the rod yields consistent results across different visiting laboratories. And the rod's length, being a solid metal bar stored under controlled conditions, is stable over long periods. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the bronze rod is subject to thermal expansion: its length varies with temperature. If the standard temperature at which the rod is to be measured is not specified, measurements taken on different days at different ambient temperatures will not be truly commensurable, despite appearing stable under the controlled conditions of the laboratory.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the progression towards better-defined units involved reducing dependence upon contingent physical circumstances — the accuracy of a particular survey, the stability of a particular artefact, the constancy of a particular astronomical motion. The definition fails to require that U be specified in terms that are invariant with respect to the environmental conditions under which the measurement is performed, so that a unit which appears stable under controlled conditions may prove unstable under the variations that actual measurement practice inevitably encounters.

background_cases: When the Copernican system displaced the Ptolemaic, the transition involved not merely a change in astronomical theory but a transformation in the fundamental categories by which celestial phenomena were understood. The very distinction between celestial and terrestrial physics, which had structured inquiry for centuries, was dissolved, and the questions that astronomers considered meaningful were altered accordingly.

When the Lavoisierian chemistry of oxygen displaced the phlogiston theory, the change involved more than the substitution of one substance for another in chemical equations. The conceptual framework within which combustion, calcination, and respiration were understood was restructured: what had been the release of phlogiston became the absorption of oxygen, and the criteria for what counted as an adequate chemical explanation were transformed.

When Darwin's theory of natural selection displaced the theory of special creation, the change was not merely a matter of replacing one causal hypothesis with another. The framework within which the diversity of life was understood was reconstituted: the question shifted from asking what purpose a species served in the divine plan to asking what selective pressures had shaped its traits.
definition: A scientific revolution has occurred when (i) a community of practitioners abandons one comprehensive theoretical framework and adopts another, (ii) the new framework explains all the phenomena that the old framework explained, and (iii) the new framework explains additional phenomena that the old framework could not explain.
conditions: A community of practitioners abandons one comprehensive theoretical framework and adopts another
The new framework explains all the phenomena that the old framework explained
The new framework explains additional phenomena that the old framework could not explain
counterexample: Suppose the transition from classical mechanics to quantum mechanics is examined under this definition. The physics community abandoned classical mechanics as a comprehensive framework and adopted quantum mechanics. Quantum mechanics explains phenomena — atomic spectra, the photoelectric effect, quantum tunnelling — that classical mechanics could not. Yet quantum mechanics does not straightforwardly explain all phenomena that classical mechanics explained: the behaviour of macroscopic rigid bodies, the trajectories of billiard balls, and the orbits of planets are not directly derived from quantum-mechanical principles but require the classical limit, an approximation that is not a simple case of the new framework explaining what the old one did. The second condition is not satisfied in any straightforward sense, yet the transition is paradigmatically revolutionary.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that a scientific revolution transforms the conceptual categories and standards of explanation, not merely the range of phenomena explained. The definition, by requiring that the new framework explain everything the old one did plus more, treats revolution as cumulative extension, whereas the actual pattern is one of conceptual restructuring in which some questions and explanatory standards of the old framework are abandoned rather than answered.

background_cases: When Darwin explained the geographical distribution of species on oceanic islands, the explanation derived its force from its identification of a mechanism — natural selection operating upon organisms that had arrived by dispersal — that connected the observed distribution to independently established biological and geological facts. The explanation did not merely describe the distribution; it showed how it arose from processes whose operation was already understood.

When Pasteur explained the souring of wine by the action of micro-organisms, the explanation drew its power from the identification of a specific causal agent whose behaviour could be independently observed and manipulated. The explanation connected the macroscopic phenomenon to a microscopic mechanism whose workings were accessible to further investigation.

When Wegener proposed that the matching coastlines of Africa and South America were explained by continental drift, the explanation gained its force from identifying a single mechanism — the movement of landmasses — that accounted for a diverse range of otherwise unrelated geological and palaeontological observations.
definition: A scientific explanation of a phenomenon P is adequate if and only if (i) it identifies a mechanism M that produces P, (ii) M is independently supported by evidence drawn from outside the phenomenon being explained, and (iii) M accounts for the specific features of P rather than merely for its general character.
conditions: The explanation identifies a mechanism M that produces P
M is independently supported by evidence from outside the phenomenon explained
M accounts for the specific features of P rather than merely its general character
counterexample: Suppose a geologist explains the formation of a particular mountain range by identifying the mechanism of tectonic plate collision, which is independently supported by seismological evidence from other regions, and which accounts for the specific features of the range, including its orientation, elevation profile, and rock composition. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the mountain range in question was formed not by a single episode of plate collision but by two distinct episodes separated by hundreds of millions of years, involving different plates moving in different directions. The mechanism of plate collision accounts for the specific features of the range only if one assumes a single collision event; the actual formation required two episodes whose combined effects are not derivable from the mechanism alone without knowledge of the specific historical sequence.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the adequacy of a mechanistic explanation depends not only upon the identification and independent support of the mechanism, but upon the mechanism's ability to account for the particular historical sequence by which the phenomenon came into being. A mechanism may be correct in type yet inadequate in detail when the phenomenon results from a complex historical sequence that the mechanism, stated in general terms, does not by itself determine.

background_cases: When Halley predicted the return of the comet that now bears his name, the prediction's impressiveness derived from the fact that it was derived from Newtonian gravitational theory before the event occurred, and the subsequent observation of the comet in 1758 confirmed a prediction that had been advanced decades in advance on the basis of the theory alone.

When Mendeleev predicted the existence and properties of then-undiscovered elements on the basis of gaps in his periodic table, the predictions were compelling because they specified in detail the properties the elements would possess, and the subsequent discovery of gallium and germanium confirmed those specifications with remarkable accuracy.

When Einstein's general relativity predicted the bending of starlight by the sun, the prediction was confirmed by Eddington's 1919 eclipse observations. The prediction was derived from the theory prior to the observation, and its confirmation was widely regarded as decisive evidence for the theory.
definition: A scientific prediction is evidentially significant if and only if (i) it is derived from a theory prior to the observation of the predicted phenomenon, (ii) it specifies the predicted outcome with sufficient precision that a range of alternative outcomes would count as disconfirming, and (iii) the predicted outcome is subsequently observed.
conditions: The prediction is derived from a theory prior to observation of the predicted phenomenon
The prediction specifies the outcome with sufficient precision to be disconfirmable
The predicted outcome is subsequently observed
counterexample: Suppose a theoretical physicist derives from her theory a precise prediction concerning the decay rate of a particular subatomic particle. The prediction is made before the relevant experiment is performed and specifies the rate with sufficient precision that a range of alternative rates would disconfirm it. The experiment is subsequently carried out and the predicted rate is observed. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the theory from which the prediction was derived contains an adjustable parameter that was set, by the theorist, to a value chosen specifically to ensure that the prediction would match the anticipated experimental result. The prediction is temporally prior to the observation, precise, and confirmed, yet its evidential significance is undermined by the fact that the theory was tuned to produce this particular prediction.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the evidential significance of a prediction depends not merely upon its temporal priority, precision, and confirmation, but upon the prediction being a genuine consequence of the theory's core principles rather than an artefact of parameter adjustment. A prediction produced by tuning a free parameter to match an expected result carries no more evidential weight than a post hoc accommodation, despite satisfying every condition of the definition.

background_cases: When the phlogiston theory was confronted with Lavoisier's oxygen experiments, the theory was modified by the introduction of the hypothesis that phlogiston possessed negative weight, thereby accommodating the observed weight gain during combustion. The modification rendered the theory immune to refutation by the very experiments that were designed to test it.

When the luminiferous aether theory was confronted with the null result of the Michelson-Morley experiment, proponents introduced the Lorentz contraction hypothesis, which accounted for the null result by positing that objects contract in their direction of motion through the aether. The modification preserved the theory from refutation but at the cost of introducing an ad hoc assumption.

When Ptolemaic astronomy was confronted with discrepancies between predicted and observed planetary positions, additional epicycles were introduced to accommodate each new discrepancy. The system could in principle accommodate any observation by adding further epicycles, and this flexibility was eventually recognised as a deficiency rather than a strength.
definition: A scientific theory T is genuinely falsifiable if and only if (i) T entails at least one observational prediction that could in principle be shown false, (ii) T has not been modified in an ad hoc manner to accommodate recalcitrant observations, and (iii) the practitioners who hold T are committed to abandoning it if its predictions are decisively refuted.
conditions: T entails at least one observational prediction that could in principle be shown false
T has not been modified in ad hoc manner to accommodate recalcitrant observations
The practitioners are committed to abandoning T if its predictions are decisively refuted
counterexample: Suppose a theory of biological inheritance entails precise predictions about the ratios of traits in offspring. The theory has not been modified in an ad hoc manner. And its practitioners are committed to abandoning it if its predictions are refuted. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the theory's predictions are tested on organisms whose reproductive cycles span decades, and the sample sizes required for decisive confirmation or refutation are so large as to be practically unattainable within any reasonable period. The theory is falsifiable in principle, unmodified, and held by practitioners of genuine intellectual honesty, yet it cannot in practice be subjected to the decisive test that falsifiability is meant to ensure.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the force of falsifiability as a demarcation criterion depends upon the practical possibility of carrying out the requisite tests within a time frame that permits the scientific community to act upon the results. A theory that is falsifiable in principle but untestable in practice occupies the same functional position as one that has been immunised against refutation by ad hoc modifications.

Suppose a theory of inheritance entails precise predictions about trait ratios in offspring. The theory has not been modified in an ad hoc manner, and its proponents are committed to abandoning it if its predictions are refuted. All three conditions are met. Yet the organisms in question have reproductive cycles spanning decades, and the sample sizes needed for decisive confirmation require centuries of observation. The theory is falsifiable in principle, unmodified, and honestly held, yet it cannot in practice be subjected to the decisive test that the criterion of falsifiability is meant to ensure. What the background cases reveal is that the force of falsifiability depends upon the practical possibility of testing within a time frame that permits the community to act upon the results. A theory untestable in practice is functionally indistinguishable from one that has been immunised by ad hoc modification.

background_cases: When Galileo reported observing the moons of Jupiter through his telescope, the reliability of the observation was contested because the telescope was a novel instrument whose optical properties were not fully understood. The observation was accepted only after other astronomers replicated it using instruments of comparable quality.

When Robert Hooke reported observing the cellular structure of cork through his microscope, the observation's acceptance depended upon the capacity of other microscopists to see the same structures using similar instruments. The observation became part of accepted science when its replicability was established.

When the Geiger-Marsden experiment revealed the backscattering of alpha particles, suggesting the existence of a concentrated atomic nucleus, the observation was accepted because the experimental apparatus was well understood and the results could be reproduced by other laboratories using comparable equipment.
definition: An observation O is scientifically reliable if and only if (i) O is produced by an instrument or procedure whose operating principles are well understood, (ii) O is replicable by independent investigators using comparable instruments or procedures, and (iii) O is consistent with the body of previously accepted observations in the relevant domain.
conditions: O is produced by an instrument whose operating principles are well understood
O is replicable by independent investigators
O is consistent with previously accepted observations in the relevant domain
counterexample: Suppose an astronomer using a well-understood telescope observes a faint object in a region of the sky where no object has previously been catalogued. The observation is replicated by independent astronomers using comparable instruments. However, the observation is inconsistent with the body of previously accepted observations, for the catalogues indicate that no object should be visible at that position. All three conditions are not fully satisfied, as the third condition fails. Yet the object is a previously undetected asteroid, and the observation is entirely reliable. The inconsistency with prior observations reflects not a deficiency of the observation but a gap in the prior catalogue. By the proposed definition, the most scientifically important observations — those that reveal something genuinely new — are penalised precisely because they are inconsistent with existing records.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the requirement of consistency with prior observations is appropriate only insofar as the prior observations are themselves complete and reliable. When an observation reveals a genuinely novel phenomenon, inconsistency with the prior body of observations is not a mark of unreliability but of discovery. The definition, by imposing consistency as a condition of reliability, systematically disadvantages the very observations that are most scientifically valuable: those that extend or correct the existing body of accepted facts.
passage:
The most instructive episodes in the history of observational science reveal a common requirement and a common temptation. When Galileo reported the moons of Jupiter, the observation was accepted only when other astronomers could replicate it. When Hooke described the cellular structure of cork, acceptance depended upon replicability. When the Geiger-Marsden experiment revealed nuclear backscattering, the results were accepted because they could be reproduced in other laboratories.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an observation is scientifically reliable when it is produced by a well-understood instrument, is replicable by independent investigators, and is consistent with the body of previously accepted observations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from observational astronomy. Suppose an astronomer using a well-understood telescope observes a faint object where no object has previously been catalogued. The observation is replicated by independent astronomers. But the observation is inconsistent with accepted catalogues, which indicate no object at that position. By the proposed definition, the observation fails the third condition and is unreliable. Yet the object is a previously undetected asteroid, and the observation is entirely sound. The inconsistency reflects a gap in the prior catalogue, not a deficiency of the observation. What the background cases reveal is that consistency with prior observations is a reasonable heuristic only when those prior observations are themselves complete. The definition, by imposing consistency as a condition of reliability, penalises the very observations that are most scientifically valuable: those that reveal genuinely novel phenomena.

definition: A theory is scientifically meaningful if and only if it makes specific predictions that could, in principle, be shown to be false by observation, such that a definitive observational test exists for its central claims.
conditions: The theory makes specific predictions
These predictions could in principle be shown false by observation
A definitive observational test exists for the theory's central claims
background_cases: Newtonian mechanics predicted the precise position of planets; the discovery of Neptune confirmed a specific prediction that could have been falsified had no planet been found at the calculated location.

The phlogiston theory of combustion predicted that metals should lose weight upon calcination; when Lavoisier demonstrated that they gain weight, the prediction was falsified and the theory was abandoned.
Darwin's theory of evolution predicted that transitional fossils should exist; specific predictions about the character of such fossils could in principle be falsified by the palaeontological record.
counterexample: Consider a case in which the theory of continental drift, as proposed by Wegener in the early twentieth century, made the general prediction that continents had once been joined and had since moved apart. At the time of its proposal, no definitive observational test existed; the mechanism of continental movement was unknown and the technology to detect plate motion had not been developed. The theory made specific predictions in a broad sense, and these predictions could in principle be shown false, yet no definitive test was available for decades. By the analysis, the theory was not scientifically meaningful during this period. Yet it proved to be among the most important and ultimately well-confirmed theories in the earth sciences. The predictions it made were confirmed only once the requisite technology and theoretical framework for plate tectonics emerged.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that scientific meaningfulness requires the immediate availability of a definitive observational test, yet the case of continental drift reveals that a theory may be genuinely scientific and ultimately confirmable even when the means of testing it do not yet exist. Inductive generalisation from cases of readily testable theories leads to an overly restrictive criterion that would exclude theories whose predictions are, as it were, ahead of the available technology.
passage:
It has been proposed, on the basis of several well-known episodes in the history of science, that a theory is scientifically meaningful only if it makes predictions that could be decisively falsified by observation. One may observe that this criterion is supported by notable cases. When Newtonian mechanics was used to predict the existence and position of the planet Neptune, a definitive test was available: either a planet would be found at the calculated location or it would not. The phlogiston theory of combustion likewise made a specific prediction about the weight of calcinated metals, a prediction that Lavoisier was able to falsify through careful measurement. Similarly, Darwin's theory of evolution generated predictions about the existence of transitional forms in the fossil record, predictions that could in principle be confounded by the palaeontological evidence. From these cases, one might inductively generalise that scientific meaningfulness requires the availability of a definitive observational test.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this generalisation without qualification. Let us imagine the situation of Alfred Wegener, who in the early decades of the twentieth century proposed that the continents had once formed a single landmass and had since drifted apart. Wegener marshalled evidence from the matching coastlines of Africa and South America, from similarities in fossil assemblages, and from geological correspondences. Her theory made predictions, to be sure, but no definitive observational test existed at the time, for the mechanism of continental movement was unknown and the technology to detect it had not been invented. By the criterion abstracted from our earlier cases, Wegener's theory was not scientifically meaningful during the period of its initial proposal. It appears, however, that this judgement is plainly wrong. The theory was not idle speculation; it organised a vast body of disparate evidence and pointed towards truths that would be decisively confirmed once plate tectonics was understood. We are thus led to recognise that the inductive inference from cases of readily testable theories to a general requirement of immediate testability is too restrictive, and that a theory may be genuinely scientific even when the means of testing its predictions have not yet been devised.

definition: A scientific revolution occurs when a new paradigm replaces an old one, and the replacement is rational if and only if the new paradigm solves all the problems the old paradigm solved while additionally solving problems the old paradigm could not.
conditions: A new paradigm replaces an old one
The new paradigm solves all the problems the old paradigm solved
The new paradigm additionally solves problems the old paradigm could not
background_cases: The replacement of Ptolemaic geocentrism by Copernican heliocentrism was held to be rational because the new system explained planetary retrograde motion more naturally while retaining the predictive capacity of the old.

The replacement of Newtonian mechanics by Einsteinian relativity was regarded as rational because relativity explained the precession of Mercury's perihelion while preserving Newtonian predictions at low velocities.

The transition from classical to quantum mechanics was justified because quantum theory explained blackbody radiation and the photoelectric effect while recovering classical results in the appropriate limit.
counterexample: Suppose that one considers the replacement of classical Newtonian optics by the early wave theory of light proposed by Fresnel. The wave theory solved problems the corpuscular theory could not, such as diffraction and interference. However, the corpuscular theory had offered a straightforward explanation of the sharp shadows cast by opaque objects, an explanation that the early wave theory handled only with difficulty and by means of elaborate mathematical apparatus. The new paradigm did not solve all the problems of the old equally well; it traded strengths in one area for advantages in another. Yet the transition was widely regarded as scientifically rational.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that paradigm replacement is rational only when the new paradigm is strictly superior, solving everything the old one solved and more. Yet the case of the wave theory of light reveals that actual scientific revolutions involve trade-offs, where the new paradigm excels in some respects while initially struggling with problems the old paradigm handled with ease. Inductive generalisation from cases of clean superiority leads to an idealised picture that fails to capture the messier rationality of real scientific change.
passage:
One may consider several prominent episodes in the history of science in order to determine what makes the replacement of one paradigm by another a rational undertaking. In the case of the Copernican revolution, the heliocentric system was taken to be rationally superior to the Ptolemaic because it explained the retrograde motion of the planets more naturally while retaining the predictive scope of its predecessor. When Einstein's relativity replaced Newtonian mechanics, the new framework accounted for the anomalous precession of Mercury's orbit while preserving Newtonian results as a limiting case. Similarly, the advent of quantum mechanics was justified by its ability to explain phenomena such as blackbody radiation and the photoelectric effect while recovering the predictions of classical physics in the appropriate domain. From these cases, one might inductively conclude that a rational paradigm shift occurs precisely when the new theory solves all the problems of the old and additional ones besides.

Yet we should hesitate to suppose that this generalisation holds without exception. Let us consider the replacement of Newton's corpuscular theory of light by Fresnel's wave theory in the early nineteenth century. The wave theory provided elegant explanations of diffraction and interference, phenomena that the corpuscular theory could accommodate only with difficulty. However, the corpuscular theory had offered a simple and intuitive account of the sharp shadows cast by opaque objects, an account that the wave theory could match only through complex mathematical constructions involving the superposition of infinitely many wavelets. The new paradigm did not straightforwardly solve all the problems its predecessor had managed; rather, it traded ease of explanation in one area for superiority in another. Yet few would deny that the transition to the wave theory was rational. We are thus led to conclude that the rational replacement of paradigms need not require strict superiority across every problem domain, and that the inductive generalisation drawn from our initial cases presents an idealised picture that does not faithfully represent the character of actual scientific revolutions.

definition: An experiment is a crucial test between two competing hypotheses if and only if it is designed so that one hypothesis predicts outcome A while the other predicts outcome B, the experiment is performed under controlled conditions, and the observed outcome decisively eliminates one hypothesis.
conditions: One hypothesis predicts outcome A while the other predicts outcome B
The experiment is performed under controlled conditions
The observed outcome decisively eliminates one hypothesis
background_cases: Pasteur's swan-neck flask experiment was regarded as a crucial test between spontaneous generation and biogenesis: one hypothesis predicted microbial growth in the open flask while the other did not, and the result decisively favoured biogenesis.

Eddington's 1919 solar eclipse observation was presented as a crucial test between Newtonian and Einsteinian predictions for the bending of starlight, with the measured deflection matching Einstein's prediction and eliminating Newton's.
counterexample: Consider a case in which the Michelson-Morley experiment of 1887 was designed to detect the motion of the Earth through the luminiferous aether. One hypothesis, the stationary aether theory, predicted a measurable fringe shift, while the alternative predicted none. The experiment was performed under carefully controlled conditions and yielded a null result. Yet the stationary aether hypothesis was not decisively eliminated; instead, defenders introduced the Lorentz contraction hypothesis to account for the null result within an aether framework. The experiment, supposedly crucial, did not in fact eliminate either hypothesis decisively, because auxiliary hypotheses could be modified to absorb the anomalous result.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that crucial experiments can decisively eliminate one of two competing hypotheses. Yet the Michelson-Morley case reveals that the elimination is never of a hypothesis in isolation but of a hypothesis together with its auxiliary assumptions. When auxiliaries can be modified, no single experiment is truly decisive. Inductive generalisation from apparently decisive tests obscures the holistic character of empirical testing.
passage:
The notion of a crucial experiment, an experiment capable of deciding between two rival hypotheses, has played a prominent role in the methodology of science. One may observe that Pasteur's celebrated swan-neck flask experiment appears to be just such a test. The hypothesis of spontaneous generation predicted that microbial life would arise in nutrient broth exposed to air, while the hypothesis of biogenesis predicted that it would not arise so long as airborne microorganisms were excluded. The experiment was carefully controlled, and the result, the absence of growth in the swan-neck flask, was taken to eliminate spontaneous generation decisively. Similarly, Eddington's observation of starlight deflection during the solar eclipse of 1919 was presented as a crucial test between the Newtonian and Einsteinian predictions for the bending of light near the sun; the observed deflection matched Einstein's value and was taken to eliminate the Newtonian prediction. From these cases, one might generalise that crucial experiments are possible and that they function by decisively eliminating one hypothesis in favour of its rival.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this generalisation without reservation. Let us consider the Michelson-Morley experiment of 1887, which sought to detect the Earth's motion through the supposed luminiferous aether. The stationary aether hypothesis predicted a detectable fringe shift in an interferometer, while the hypothesis that no such motion was detectable predicted a null result. The experiment was performed with great care and yielded the null result. It might appear that the aether hypothesis was thereby eliminated. However, proponents of the aether were not compelled to abandon their position. Lorentz and others proposed that bodies moving through the aether undergo a physical contraction that precisely cancels the expected fringe shift. This auxiliary hypothesis preserved the aether framework in the face of the null result. The experiment, far from being decisive, could be accommodated by either side through the adjustment of auxiliary assumptions. We are led to conclude that the inductive inference from apparently crucial experiments to the general possibility of decisive tests overlooks the holistic character of empirical testing: it is never a single hypothesis that faces the tribunal of experiment, but a network of hypotheses and auxiliaries together.

definition: A novel prediction confirms a theory more strongly than the accommodation of already known data if and only if the prediction is derived from the theory before the relevant observation, the prediction is specific and precise, and the successful prediction could not have been engineered by adjusting the theory after the fact.
conditions: The prediction is derived from the theory before the relevant observation
The prediction is specific and precise
The successful prediction could not have been engineered by adjusting the theory after the fact
background_cases: Einstein's general relativity predicted the precise degree of bending of starlight by the sun before Eddington's observation, and this novel prediction was taken as strong confirmation of the theory.
Mendeleev's periodic table predicted the existence and properties of undiscovered elements such as gallium and germanium before their discovery, powerfully confirming the periodic law.
The standard model of particle physics predicted the existence of the Higgs boson with specific properties decades before its detection at the Large Hadron Collider.
counterexample: Suppose that a cosmological theory is devised in 1990 and, among its many consequences, it entails that the expansion of the universe is accelerating. In 1998, observations of distant supernovae reveal that the expansion is indeed accelerating. The prediction was derived before the observation, it was specific, and it could not have been engineered after the fact. Yet the theorist had devised the theory by exploring a vast space of possible models and had published the one that happened to include this consequence among hundreds of alternative models she had also constructed. The successful prediction, though temporally novel, was the result of a strategy that virtually guaranteed that some model would match any future observation. The prediction satisfies all three conditions yet provides far weaker confirmation than the background cases suggest.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that temporal novelty, specificity, and non-ad-hoc derivation are sufficient for strong confirmation. Yet the cosmological case reveals that when a theorist generates sufficiently many models, the appearance of successful novel prediction can be achieved without genuine theoretical insight. Inductive generalisation from cases of principled prediction to a general criterion of confirmation overlooks the problem of selection effects and the multiplication of theoretical variants.
passage:
It has long been maintained that the successful novel prediction of a phenomenon provides stronger confirmation of a theory than the mere accommodation of data already known. Several celebrated episodes appear to support this view. Einstein's prediction of the precise degree of starlight deflection near the sun, confirmed by Eddington's observations, was widely regarded as powerful confirmation of general relativity precisely because the prediction was made before the measurement. Mendeleev's prediction of the properties of as-yet-undiscovered elements, later confirmed by the isolation of gallium and germanium, was taken as strong evidence for the periodic law. The prediction of the Higgs boson, with its specific mass range and decay properties, decades before its detection, was held to be among the great confirmatory successes of modern physics. From these cases, one might inductively conclude that a prediction which is temporally novel, specific, and not engineered after the fact provides strong confirmation of the theory from which it is derived.

Yet we should hesitate to suppose that these conditions are sufficient in all circumstances. Let us imagine a cosmologist who, in 1990, explores a vast landscape of possible theoretical models. She constructs hundreds of variants, each entailing different consequences for the large-scale behaviour of the universe. Among these, one model predicts that the expansion of the universe is accelerating. In 1998, observations of distant supernovae reveal that the expansion is indeed accelerating, and the cosmologist points to her model as having made a successful novel prediction. The prediction was derived before the observation, it was specific, and it was not adjusted after the fact. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, it appears that the confirmation afforded by this prediction is far weaker than in our paradigmatic cases, for the theorist's strategy of generating a multitude of models virtually guaranteed that at least one would match any future observation. The success is an artefact of selection rather than of theoretical insight. We are thus led to recognise that the inductive generalisation from cases of principled prediction to a sufficient criterion of confirmation overlooks the problem of theoretical proliferation and selection effects.
